This specification defines the authoritative protocol requirements, based on the TypeScript schema in [schema.ts]

(https://www.jsonrpc.org/) 2.0 messages to establish communication between:
**Hosts** : LLM applications that initiate connections
**Clients** : Connectors within the host application
**Servers** : Services that provide context and capabilities

Servers offer any of the following features to clients:
**Resources** : Context and data, for the user or the AI model to use
**Prompts** : Templated messages and workflows for users
**Tools** : Functions for the AI model to execute

Clients may offer the following features to servers:
**Sampling** : Server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions
**Roots** : Server-initiated inquiries into URI or filesystem boundaries to operate in
**Elicitation** : Server-initiated requests for additional information from users

excerpts:
**Orchestration and control flow.** Loops, branches, concurrency, retries, timeouts, and "this step must happen after that step." This is where agentic systems stop being prompts and become software.
**State management.** Not chat history. Actual execution state: where you are in the workflow, what tools were called, what outputs were produced, and how to resume after failure or interruption. Frameworks like LangGraph explicitly sell [durable execution](https://changelog.langchain.com/announcements/langgraph-1-0-is-now-generally-available) as a core capability here.
[LangGraph](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview) treats agent behavior as a graph and state machine. You explicitly define nodes and edges, then get infrastructure for long-running workflows, [durable execution](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/durable-execution) , and human oversight patterns.
**Observability and debugging.** Tracing tool calls, understanding why the agent took a particular path, and reproducing failures. Agents fail probabilistically; your debugging tooling needs to assume that. See our developer's guide to tracing agentic applications for a deeper look at what this means in practice.

excerpts: **GAIA (General AI Agent benchmark):** GAIA is a broad benchmark introduced in late 2023 as a collaboration between academia and industry (Meta AI, Hugging Face, and others) ( ( [en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manus_(AI_agent)#:~:text=Manus%20achieved%20notable%20performance%20on,solving.%5B%2015%20%5D%5B%2016) ) ). It presents a series of complex tasks that require **multi-step reasoning, tool use, web browsing, and even interpreting different data modalities** – essentially a gauntlet of real-world problems. GAIA is structured in **three difficulty levels** ( ( [encorp.ai](https://encorp.ai/en/blog/gaia-ai-intelligence-benchmark_2025-04-19?trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block#:~:text=GAIA%20assesses%20AI%20models%20across,three%20difficulty%20levels) ) ): Level 1 has simple one-tool tasks, Level 2 involves intermediate multi-tool tasks, and Level 3 includes the most complex scenarios (e.g. requiring extensive planning and use of numerous tools).
GAIA’s significance is that it tests a wide range of capabilities in one benchmark.
By 2026, we may have **new benchmarks** focusing on multi-agent cooperation, or on long-term tasks (like an agent that works continuously for a week on a complex project).

**Native MCP integration** supports four server types: stdio (local processes), SSE (server-sent events), HTTP, and notably in-process SDK MCP servers that eliminate subprocess overhead entirely [4][5]
**MCP standardization** has eliminated the fragmentation that plagued earlier integration approaches, with unanimous adoption across major AI providers.
**Long-running task support** through a two-agent architecture (initializer + coding agent) enables sustained focus across multiple context windows — Claude Sonnet 4.5 demonstrated **30+ hours of autonomous coding** in testing [6]
Anthropic published detailed engineering guidance on November 26, 2025, documenting patterns for effective long-running agent harnesses including progress tracking files, git integration for state management, and structured handoff artifacts between sessions [6].
OpenAI launched its Agents SDK in **March 2025** as a production successor to the experimental Swarm framework from 2024 [8].
**Multi-agent handoffs** function as first-class primitives rather than afterthoughts. In version 0.6.0 (November 2025), a breaking change collapsed message history into a single context message during handoffs, packaging prior conversation with the header: “For context, here is the conversation so far between the user and the previous agent” [9].
**Automatic context compaction** summarizes conversation history when approaching the 200k token limit, with a reported **39% performance improvement** on agentic search tasks when combined with memory tools [1]
**Hybrid architectures have become the production standard.** As LangChain states: “Most agentic systems in production are a combination of workflows and agents.
The Assistants API never left beta and is scheduled for sunset on August 26, 2026, with full migration guidance provided in official documentation.

excerpts: **GAIA (General AI Agent benchmark):** GAIA is a broad benchmark introduced in late 2023 as a collaboration between academia and industry (Meta AI, Hugging Face, and others) ( ( [en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manus_(AI_agent)#:~:text=Manus%20achieved%20notable%20performance%20on,solving.%5B%2015%20%5D%5B%2016) ) ). It presents a series of complex tasks that require **multi-step reasoning, tool use, web browsing, and even interpreting different data modalities** – essentially a gauntlet of real-world problems. GAIA is structured in **three difficulty levels** ( ( [encorp.ai](https://encorp.ai/en/blog/gaia-ai-intelligence-benchmark_2025-04-19?trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block#:~:text=GAIA%20assesses%20AI%20models%20across,three%20difficulty%20levels) ) ): Level 1 has simple one-tool tasks, Level 2 involves intermediate multi-tool tasks, and Level 3 includes the most complex scenarios (e.g. requiring extensive planning and use of numerous tools).
GAIA’s significance is that it tests a wide range of capabilities in one benchmark.
By 2026, we may have **new benchmarks** focusing on multi-agent cooperation, or on long-term tasks (like an agent that works continuously for a week on a complex project).

excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. It provides a universal interface for AI systems to:

**Read data** from files, databases, and APIs
**Execute functions** through defined tools
**Handle contextual prompts** with templates and workflows
MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI models connect to external data sources and tools—think "USB-C for AI."
Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all support MCP, making it the de-facto industry standard.
Over 13,000 MCP servers launched on GitHub in 2025, covering everything from databases to Slack to custom APIs.
Building an MCP server takes less than 100 lines of code, and the protocol handles authentication, capabilities negotiation, and error handling.
Security requires explicit user consent for all operations—MCP doesn't grant blanket access.

This specification defines the authoritative protocol requirements, based on the TypeScript schema in [schema.ts]

(https://www.jsonrpc.org/) 2.0 messages to establish communication between:
**Hosts** : LLM applications that initiate connections
**Clients** : Connectors within the host application
**Servers** : Services that provide context and capabilities

Servers offer any of the following features to clients:
**Resources** : Context and data, for the user or the AI model to use
**Prompts** : Templated messages and workflows for users
**Tools** : Functions for the AI model to execute

Clients may offer the following features to servers:
**Sampling** : Server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions
**Roots** : Server-initiated inquiries into URI or filesystem boundaries to operate in
**Elicitation** : Server-initiated requests for additional information from users

excerpts:
Tool integrations are where you burn time. [MCP](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol) is an open standard for connecting assistants to the systems where data and capabilities live. What matters for framework choice in 2026 is less "does it have 30 built-in tools" and more: does it support MCP cleanly, and does it help you manage auth, lifecycle, and failures of tool providers?
[OpenAI's Agents SDK supports MCP servers directly](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/mcp/) and documents MCP as a standard way to provide context and tools to agents. [Google ADK provides built-in MCP support](https://google.github.io/adk-docs/mcp/) including examples for connecting to external MCP servers. [Agno's runtime exposes MCP](https://docs.agno.com/introduction) as an optional capability at the runtime layer, reflecting that MCP is becoming infrastructure, not a niche add-on.
[LangGraph](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview) treats agent behavior as a graph and state machine. You explicitly define nodes and edges, then get infrastructure for long-running workflows, [durable execution](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/durable-execution) , and human oversight patterns.
[CrewAI](https://docs.crewai.com/en/concepts/production-architecture) pushes a "Flow-first" mindset for production. You can run individual agents or crews, but the recommended production architecture wraps them in a Flow to provide structure and scalability. Orchestration as a first-class product concept, not just code structure.
[OpenAI's Agents SDK](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents-sdk) and [Anthropic's Agent SDK](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/overview) sit closer to the "minimal loop" end: few abstractions, tool use plus context management, and you compose the rest.
**Orchestration and control flow.** Loops, branches, concurrency, retries, timeouts, and "this step must happen after that step." This is where agentic systems stop being prompts and become software.
**State management.** Not chat history. Actual execution state: where you are in the workflow, what tools were called, what outputs were produced, and how to resume after failure or interruption. Frameworks like LangGraph explicitly sell [durable execution](https://changelog.langchain.com/announcements/langgraph-1-0-is-now-generally-available) as a core capability here.
**Memory management.** Short-term working memory (what the agent needs right now) versus long-term memory (what should persist across sessions). If you don't define this deliberately, your system becomes either forgetful or bloated and unstable.
**Tool ecosystem and interfaces.** Defining tools, validating schemas, handling auth, and connecting to external systems. Increasingly, the tool ecosystem is less about built-in connectors and more about supporting an open interface like [MCP (Model Context Protocol)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18) . MCP's whole point is to standardize how assistants connect to tools and data sources, and its adoption across frameworks has been rapid.
**Observability and debugging.** Tracing tool calls, understanding why the agent took a particular path, and reproducing failures. Agents fail probabilistically; your debugging tooling needs to assume that. See our developer's guide to tracing agentic applications for a deeper look at what this means in practice.
**Human-in-the-loop and guardrails.** Approval gates, review steps, and the ability to pause and resume safely, especially for anything that changes data or triggers real-world actions.

excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. It provides a universal interface for AI systems to:

**Read data** from files, databases, and APIs
**Execute functions** through defined tools
**Handle contextual prompts** with templates and workflows
MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI models connect to external data sources and tools—think "USB-C for AI."
Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all support MCP, making it the de-facto industry standard.
Over 13,000 MCP servers launched on GitHub in 2025, covering everything from databases to Slack to custom APIs.
Building an MCP server takes less than 100 lines of code, and the protocol handles authentication, capabilities negotiation, and error handling.
Security requires explicit user consent for all operations—MCP doesn't grant blanket access.

excerpts:
In 2025, coding agents moved from experimental tools to production systems
that ship real features to real customers.

In 2026, we predict these gains will extend far beyond incremental
improvements to existing tools or models.

We expect single agents to become
coordinated teams of agents.

Tasks that took hours or days may now be
completed with minimal human intervention.

Engineering teams discovered that
AI can now handle entire implementation workflows: writing tests, debugging
failures, generating documentation, and navigating increasingly complex
codebases.

Research from our
Societal Impacts team reveals that while developers use AI in roughly 60% of
their work, they report being able to "fully delegate" only 0-20% of tasks.

Trend 8: Dual-use risk requires security-first
architecture

Trend 2
Single agents evolve into coordinated teams

Organizations adopt
multi-agent workflows that maximize performance gains through parallel
reasoning across separate context windows.

Fountain , a frontline workforce management platform, achieved 50%
faster screening, 40% quicker onboarding, and 2x candidate conversions
using Claude for hierarchical multi-agent orchestration.

Their Fountain Copilot serves as the central orchestration agent to
coordinate specialized sub-agents for candidate screening, automated
document generation, and sentiment analysis.

Trend 4
Human oversight scales through intelligent
collaboration

gents
learning when to ask for help, rather than blindly attempting every task, and
humans stepping into the loop only when required.

Organizations use AI agents
to review large-scale AI-generated output, analyzing code for security
vulnerabilities, architectural consistency, and quality issues

Trend 3
Long-running agents build complete systems

Agents evolve from
handling discrete tasks that complete in minutes to working autonomously for
extended periods, building and testing entire applications and systems with
periodic human checkpoints.

Claude Code finished the entire job
in seven hours of autonomous work in a single run.

Priorities for the year ahead
These eight trends are poised to define agentic coding in 2026 all converge on
a central theme: software development is shifting from an activity centered on
writing code to an activity grounded in orchestrating agents that write code—
while maintaining the human judgment, oversight, and collaboration that
ensures quality outcomes

1. Mastering multi-agent coordination to handle complexity that single-agent
systems cannot address

2. Scaling human-agent oversight through AI-automated review systems that
focus human attention where it matters most

3. Extending agentic coding beyond engineering to empower domain experts
across departments

4. Embedding security architecture as a part of agentic system design from the
earliest stages

excerpts:
[LangGraph](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview) treats agent behavior as a graph and state machine. You explicitly define nodes and edges, then get infrastructure for long-running workflows, [durable execution](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/durable-execution) , and human oversight patterns.
**Orchestration and control flow.** Loops, branches, concurrency, retries, timeouts, and "this step must happen after that step." This is where agentic systems stop being prompts and become software.
**State management.** Not chat history. Actual execution state: where you are in the workflow, what tools were called, what outputs were produced, and how to resume after failure or interruption. Frameworks like LangGraph explicitly sell [durable execution](https://changelog.langchain.com/announcements/langgraph-1-0-is-now-generally-available) as a core capability here.
**Observability and debugging.** Tracing tool calls, understanding why the agent took a particular path, and reproducing failures. Agents fail probabilistically; your debugging tooling needs to assume that. See our developer's guide to tracing agentic applications for a deeper look at what this means in practice.

excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. It provides a universal interface for AI systems to:

**Read data** from files, databases, and APIs
**Execute functions** through defined tools
**Handle contextual prompts** with templates and workflows
MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI models connect to external data sources and tools—think "USB-C for AI."
Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all support MCP, making it the de-facto industry standard.
Over 13,000 MCP servers launched on GitHub in 2025, covering everything from databases to Slack to custom APIs.
Building an MCP server takes less than 100 lines of code, and the protocol handles authentication, capabilities negotiation, and error handling.
Security requires explicit user consent for all operations—MCP doesn't grant blanket access.

**Native MCP integration** supports four server types: stdio (local processes), SSE (server-sent events), HTTP, and notably in-process SDK MCP servers that eliminate subprocess overhead entirely [4][5]

excerpts: **GAIA (General AI Agent benchmark):** GAIA is a broad benchmark introduced in late 2023 as a collaboration between academia and industry (Meta AI, Hugging Face, and others) ( ( [en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manus_(AI_agent)#:~:text=Manus%20achieved%20notable%20performance%20on,solving.%5B%2015%20%5D%5B%2016) ) ). It presents a series of complex tasks that require **multi-step reasoning, tool use, web browsing, and even interpreting different data modalities** – essentially a gauntlet of real-world problems. GAIA is structured in **three difficulty levels** ( ( [encorp.ai](https://encorp.ai/en/blog/gaia-ai-intelligence-benchmark_2025-04-19?trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block#:~:text=GAIA%20assesses%20AI%20models%20across,three%20difficulty%20levels) ) ): Level 1 has simple one-tool tasks, Level 2 involves intermediate multi-tool tasks, and Level 3 includes the most complex scenarios (e.g. requiring extensive planning and use of numerous tools).
GAIA’s significance is that it tests a wide range of capabilities in one benchmark.
By 2026, we may have **new benchmarks** focusing on multi-agent cooperation, or on long-term tasks (like an agent that works continuously for a week on a complex project).

excerpts:
[LangGraph](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview) treats agent behavior as a graph and state machine. You explicitly define nodes and edges, then get infrastructure for long-running workflows, [durable execution](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/durable-execution) , and human oversight patterns.
**State management.** Not chat history. Actual execution state: where you are in the workflow, what tools were called, what outputs were produced, and how to resume after failure or interruption. Frameworks like LangGraph explicitly sell [durable execution](https://changelog.langchain.com/announcements/langgraph-1-0-is-now-generally-available) as a core capability here.
**Observability and debugging.** Tracing tool calls, understanding why the agent took a particular path, and reproducing failures. Agents fail probabilistically; your debugging tooling needs to assume that. See our developer's guide to tracing agentic applications for a deeper look at what this means in practice.
**Orchestration and control flow.** Loops, branches, concurrency, retries, timeouts, and "this step must happen after that step." This is where agentic systems stop being prompts and become software.

excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. It provides a universal interface for AI systems to:

**Read data** from files, databases, and APIs
**Execute functions** through defined tools
**Handle contextual prompts** with templates and workflows
MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI models connect to external data sources and tools—think "USB-C for AI."
Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all support MCP, making it the de-facto industry standard.
Over 13,000 MCP servers launched on GitHub in 2025, covering everything from databases to Slack to custom APIs.
Building an MCP server takes less than 100 lines of code, and the protocol handles authentication, capabilities negotiation, and error handling.
Security requires explicit user consent for all operations—MCP doesn't grant blanket access.
citations.title: 🤖 MCP Protocol Guide 2026: Connect AI to Any - ToLearn | ToLearn Blog

excerpts: odelcontextprotocol.io) (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools
This specification defines the authoritative protocol requirements, based on the TypeScript schema in [schema.ts]

(https://www.jsonrpc.org/) 2.0 messages to establish communication between:
**Hosts** : LLM applications that initiate connections
**Clients** : Connectors within the host application
**Servers** : Services that provide context and capabilities

Servers offer any of the following features to clients:
**Resources** : Context and data, for the user or the AI model to use
**Prompts** : Templated messages and workflows for users
**Tools** : Functions for the AI model to execute

Clients may offer the following features to servers:
**Sampling** : Server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions
**Roots** : Server-initiated inquiries into URI or filesystem boundaries to operate in
**Elicitation** : Server-initiated requests for additional information from users

**Memory management.** Short-term working memory (what the agent needs right now) versus long-term memory (what should persist across sessions). If you don't define this deliberately, your system becomes either forgetful or bloated and unstable.

excerpts: **GAIA (General AI Agent benchmark):** GAIA is a broad benchmark introduced in late 2023 as a collaboration between academia and industry (Meta AI, Hugging Face, and others) ( ( [en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manus_(AI_agent)#:~:text=Manus%20achieved%20notable%20performance%20on,solving.%5B%2015%20%5D%5B%2016) ) ). It presents a series of complex tasks that require **multi-step reasoning, tool use, web browsing, and even interpreting different data modalities** – essentially a gauntlet of real-world problems. GAIA is structured in **three difficulty levels** ( ( [encorp.ai](https://encorp.ai/en/blog/gaia-ai-intelligence-benchmark_2025-04-19?trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block#:~:text=GAIA%20assesses%20AI%20models%20across,three%20difficulty%20levels) ) ): Level 1 has simple one-tool tasks, Level 2 involves intermediate multi-tool tasks, and Level 3 includes the most complex scenarios (e.g. requiring extensive planning and use of numerous tools).
GAIA’s significance is that it tests a wide range of capabilities in one benchmark.
By 2026, we may have **new benchmarks** focusing on multi-agent cooperation, or on long-term tasks (like an agent that works continuously for a week on a complex project).

excerpts: We’re releasing a more advanced speech-to-speech model and new API capabilities including MCP server support, image input, and SIP phone calling support.
The API now supports remote MCP servers, image inputs, and phone calling through Session Initiation Protocol (SIP), making voice agents more capable through access to additional tools and context.
We’re also releasing our most advanced speech-to-speech model yet— `gpt-realtime` .
We’re reducing prices for `gpt-realtime` by 20% compared to `gpt-4o-realtime-preview` —$32 / 1M audio input tokens ($0.40 for cached input tokens) and $64 / 1M audio output tokens
We’ve also added fine-grained control for conversation context to let developers set intelligent token limits and truncate multiple turns at a time, significantly reducing cost for long sessions.
Today we’re making the Realtime API generally available with new features that enable developers and enterprises to build reliable, production-ready voice agents.

**MCP standardization** has eliminated the fragmentation that plagued earlier integration approaches, with unanimous adoption across major AI providers.

**Multi-agent handoffs** function as first-class primitives rather than afterthoughts. In version 0.6.0 (November 2025), a breaking change collapsed message history into a single context message during handoffs, packaging prior conversation with the header: “For context, here is the conversation so far between the user and the previous agent” [9].

Increasingly, the tool ecosystem is less about built-in connectors and more about supporting an open interface like [MCP (Model Context Protocol)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18) . MCP's whole point is to standardize how assistants connect to tools and data sources, and its adoption across frameworks has been rapid.
[OpenAI's Agents SDK](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents-sdk) and [Anthropic's Agent SDK](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/overview) sit closer to the "minimal loop" end: few abstractions, tool use plus context management, and you compose the rest.

excerpts:
**Native MCP integration** supports four server types: stdio (local processes), SSE (server-sent events), HTTP, and notably in-process SDK MCP servers that eliminate subprocess overhead entirely [4][5]
**MCP standardization** has eliminated the fragmentation that plagued earlier integration approaches, with unanimous adoption across major AI providers.
**Long-running task support** through a two-agent architecture (initializer + coding agent) enables sustained focus across multiple context windows — Claude Sonnet 4.5 demonstrated **30+ hours of autonomous coding** in testing [6]

This specification defines the authoritative protocol requirements, based on the TypeScript schema in [schema.ts]

(https://www.jsonrpc.org/) 2.0 messages to establish communication between:
**Hosts** : LLM applications that initiate connections
**Clients** : Connectors within the host application
**Servers** : Services that provide context and capabilities

Servers offer any of the following features to clients:
**Resources** : Context and data, for the user or the AI model to use
**Prompts** : Templated messages and workflows for users
**Tools** : Functions for the AI model to execute

Clients may offer the following features to servers:
**Sampling** : Server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions
**Roots** : Server-initiated inquiries into URI or filesystem boundaries to operate in
**Elicitation** : Server-initiated requests for additional information from users

excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard introduced by Anthropic in November 2024. It provides a universal interface for AI systems to:
MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI models connect to external data sources and tools—think "USB-C for AI."
Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all support MCP, making it the de-facto industry standard.
Over 13,000 MCP servers launched on GitHub in 2025, covering everything from databases to Slack to custom APIs.
Building an MCP server takes less than 100 lines of code, and the protocol handles authentication, capabilities negotiation, and error handling.
Security requires explicit user consent for all operations—MCP doesn't grant blanket access.
citations.title: 🤖 MCP Protocol Guide 2026: Connect AI to Any - ToLearn | ToLearn Blog

excerpts: odelcontextprotocol.io) (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools
This specification defines the authoritative protocol requirements, based on the TypeScript schema in [schema.ts]

(https://www.jsonrpc.org/) 2.0 messages to establish communication between:
**Hosts** : LLM applications that initiate connections
**Clients** : Connectors within the host application
**Servers** : Services that provide context and capabilities

Servers offer any of the following features to clients:
**Resources** : Context and data, for the user or the AI model to use
**Prompts** : Templated messages and workflows for users
**Tools** : Functions for the AI model to execute

Clients may offer the following features to servers:
**Sampling** : Server-initiated agentic behaviors and recursive LLM interactions
**Roots** : Server-initiated inquiries into URI or filesystem boundaries to operate in
**Elicitation** : Server-initiated requests for additional information from users

excerpts: **GAIA (General AI Agent benchmark):** GAIA is a broad benchmark introduced in late 2023 as a collaboration between academia and industry (Meta AI, Hugging Face, and others) ( ( [en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manus_(AI_agent)#:~:text=Manus%20achieved%20notable%20performance%20on,solving.%5B%2015%20%5D%5B%2016) ) ). It presents a series of complex tasks that require **multi-step reasoning, tool use, web browsing, and even interpreting different data modalities** – essentially a gauntlet of real-world problems. GAIA is structured in **three difficulty levels** ( ( [encorp.ai](https://encorp.ai/en/blog/gaia-ai-intelligence-benchmark_2025-04-19?trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_little-text-block#:~:text=GAIA%20assesses%20AI%20models%20across,three%20difficulty%20levels) ) ): Level 1 has simple one-tool tasks, Level 2 involves intermediate multi-tool tasks, and Level 3 includes the most complex scenarios (e.g. requiring extensive planning and use of numerous tools).
By 2026, we may have **new benchmarks** focusing on multi-agent cooperation, or on long-term tasks (like an agent that works continuously for a week on a complex project).

**Resources** : Context and data, for the user or the AI model to use

**Tools** : Functions for the AI model to execute

Clients may offer the following features to servers:

executive_summary.description: A high-level summary of the key architectural decisions and concerns that have emerged for agentic AI systems in 2025-2026, moving beyond the standard decision framework.

concern_area.description: The high-level category of the architectural concern (e.g., Interoperability, Evaluation, Security).

description.description: A detailed explanation of the specific architectural concern and why it has become critical.

impact.description: The primary impact of addressing this concern, such as improving reliability, security, or scalability.

emerging_architectural_concerns.description: A list of the new, critical architectural concerns for production agent systems in 2025-2026 that are missing from earlier, standard agentic frameworks.

purpose.description: The primary goal of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), such as standardizing integration between LLMs and external tools.

specification_version.description: The latest version of the MCP specification referenced in the research (e.g., '2025-11-25').

core_features.description: A summary of the key features offered by the protocol, such as Resources, Tools, and Prompts.

adoption_status.description: Information on its adoption across the industry, noting its status as a de facto standard supported by major AI providers.

benchmark_name.description: The name of the evaluation benchmark, such as GAIA, SWE-bench Verified, or WebArena.

description.description: A description of what the benchmark is designed to measure.

focus_area.description: The specific domain the benchmark tests, such as general reasoning, coding, or web automation.

leading_performer.description: The agent or platform that has achieved top scores on this benchmark, if mentioned.

technology.description: The name of the technology or standard used for trust and authentication (e.g., OAuth/OIDC, DIDs, VCs).

description.description: An explanation of how the technology is applied in the context of agent-to-agent interactions.

use_case.description: The specific problem it solves, such as securing tool calls, verifying agent identity, or ensuring content provenance.

concept.description: The core architectural concept, such as 'Durable Execution' or 'Stateful Workflows'.

description.description: An explanation of the concept and the problem it solves, like resuming interrupted tasks.

enabling_framework.description: The primary framework or technology that implements this concept, such as LangGraph.

key_feature.description: A specific feature that enables durability, like automatic state persistence or checkpointing.

framework_name.description: The name of the agentic framework (e.g., LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK).

vendor.description: The primary company or organization developing the framework.

core_philosophy.description: The guiding design principle of the framework (e.g., 'stability-first', 'minimal abstractions', 'multi-agent orchestration').

ideal_use_case.description: The type of application or problem the framework is best suited for.

major_agent_frameworks_comparison.description: A comparative analysis of the leading agentic frameworks in 2025-2026. Each item will detail a framework's core philosophy, key features, and ideal use cases. Includes LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK, Microsoft Agent Framework, Google Agent Development Kit (ADK), and CrewAI.

pattern_name.description: The name of the multi-agent orchestration pattern (e.g., Manager-Worker, Hierarchical, Agent Mesh).

description.description: A description of how the orchestration pattern works and coordinates multiple agents.

supporting_frameworks.description: A list of frameworks that are well-suited for implementing this pattern (e.g., CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph).

pattern_name.description: The name of the collaboration pattern, such as 'Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) Checkpoint' or 'AI-Assisted Review'.

description.description: An explanation of how the pattern facilitates human oversight or interaction with the agent.

purpose.description: The goal of the pattern, such as gaining approval for high-stakes actions or scaling quality control.

modality.description: The type of data the agent interacts with (e.g., Audio, Vision, GUI).

enabling_technology.description: The specific API or capability that supports this modality, such as 'OpenAI Realtime API' or 'Computer Use'.

capability_provided.description: The function this technology enables, like 'low-latency speech-to-speech' or 'graphical user interface interaction'.

strategy_name.description: The name of the reasoning technique (e.g., Tree-of-Thought, Reflection, Graph-of-Thought).

description.description: A brief explanation of how the strategy works to improve agent planning and problem-solving.

benefit.description: The primary advantage of using this strategy, such as improved reliability, deeper reasoning, or error correction.

technique.description: The method used for enforcement, such as 'JSON Schema Constraint' or 'Function Calling'.

provider_feature.description: The specific feature name from a provider, like 'OpenAI Structured Outputs'.

guarantee_level.description: The level of reliability provided, such as 'Valid JSON syntax' vs. 'Full schema adherence'.

architectural_role.description: The importance of this technique in production systems, such as ensuring pipeline reliability or enabling agent interoperability.

concern.description: The specific area of the agent lifecycle or marketplace (e.g., Versioning & Rollback, Agent Registry).

description.description: A description of the architectural considerations for this area.

example.description: A real-world example, such as 'OpenAI GPT Store' for marketplaces or 'LangGraph's state management' for versioning.

description.description: How the technique works to reduce latency or cost.

providers.description: A list of companies or platforms that offer this optimization feature.

concept.description: The high-level concept, either 'Autonomy Taxonomy' or 'Governance Mechanism'.

name.description: The specific name of the taxonomy or mechanism (e.g., 'L1-L5 Autonomy Levels', 'Budget Caps', 'Kill Switch').

description.description: An explanation of what it is and its role in ensuring safe agent operation.

vendor.description: The name of the major AI platform provider (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft).

architectural_vision.description: A summary of the vendor's unique strategic approach to agentic AI.

key_product_or_sdk.description: The primary developer product or SDK that embodies their vision (e.g., Agents SDK, Claude Agent SDK).

ecosystem_focus.description: The area where their strategy is most focused, such as multi-agent orchestration, enterprise governance, or security.
